question: Describe the fundamental difference between lag, lead, and match capacity strategies. Provide a simple IT example for each.
expectedPoints: Lag strategy: Capacity is added only after a demand or load increase has occurred. Example: Adding more servers after user complaints about a slow application.
Lead strategy: Capacity is added in anticipation of future demand. Example: Provisioning extra network bandwidth before a major marketing campaign launch.
Match strategy: A moderate approach where capacity is added in small increments as demand changes. Example: Gradually adding virtual machines to a cloud pool as monthly active users increase.

question: Explain why 'right-sizing' is a critical goal in IT capacity planning and identify two potential negative consequences of both under-provisioning and over-provisioning capacity.
expectedPoints: Right-sizing aims to match IT resources precisely with business demand to optimize cost and performance.
Under-provisioning consequences: 1) Poor application performance and user dissatisfaction. 2) System outages or failures during peak loads.
Over-provisioning consequences: 1) Wasted financial resources on unused hardware/software licenses. 2) Increased operational complexity and unnecessary power/space consumption.

question: Identify the two primary types of scalability in an IT context.
expectedPoints: Vertical Scalability (Scaling Up): Adding more power (e.g., CPU, RAM) to an existing single system or node.
Horizontal Scalability (Scaling Out): Adding more nodes or instances (e.g., servers) to a pool or cluster to share the load.
